rules.title: to / too / two
rules.rule: Use 'to' for direction or infinitive (go to), 'too' for also or excess (too many), and 'two' for the number 2.

rules.title: their / there / they're
rules.rule: 'Their' shows possession, 'there' is a place or intro (there is), 'they're' means they are.

rules.title: your / you're
rules.rule: 'Your' is possessive; 'you're' is a contraction of you are.

rules.title: its / it's
rules.rule: 'It's' always means it is or it has. 'Its' is possessive with no apostrophe.

rules.title: affect / effect
rules.rule: Usually 'affect' is a verb (to influence); 'effect' is often a noun (the result).

rules.title: accept / except
rules.rule: 'Accept' means to receive or agree; 'except' means excluding.

rules.title: than / then
rules.rule: Use 'than' for comparisons; 'then' for time or what happens next.

rules.title: lose / loose
rules.rule: 'Lose' means to misplace or not win; 'loose' means not tight.

rules.title: whether / weather
rules.rule: 'Whether' introduces a choice; 'weather' is climate conditions.

examples.wrong: I don't know weather to go
examples.correct: I don't know whether to go

rules.title: principal / principle
rules.rule: 'Principal' is the head of a school or main amount; 'principle' is a rule or belief.

rules.title: specific / Pacific
rules.rule: 'Specific' means particular or exact; 'Pacific' is an ocean or region. Do not use 'pacific' when you mean 'specific'.

rules.title: pacific isle → specific aisle
rules.rule: In a shopping context, 'pacific isle' is wrong: use 'specific aisle' (particular aisle). 'Isle' is an island; 'aisle' is a store walkway. 'Pacific' is not 'specific'.

rules.title: aisle / isle / I'll
rules.rule: 'Aisle' is a walkway; 'isle' is a small island; 'I'll' means I will.

rules.title: definitely
rules.rule: The correct spelling is 'definitely' (not definately or defiantly).

rules.title: irregardless / regardless
rules.rule: 'Irregardless' is nonstandard; use 'regardless' or 'regardless of'.

rules.title: could of / could have
rules.rule: After could, would, should use 'have' not 'of' (could have / could've).

rules.title: literally
rules.rule: Use 'literally' only for factual truth; avoid when you mean figuratively.

rules.title: less / fewer
rules.rule: Use 'fewer' with countable nouns; 'less' with uncountable amounts.

rules.title: who / whom
rules.rule: Use 'who' as subject; 'whom' as object (To whom did you speak?).

examples.wrong: Who did you give it to?
examples.correct: Whom did you give it to?

rules.title: a lot
rules.rule: 'A lot' is two words; 'allot' means to distribute.

rules.title: people / peoples
rules.rule: Use 'people' for plural persons; 'peoples' only for ethnic/national groups. Never 'peoples packages' for shopping.

rules.title: comma splice / run-on
rules.rule: Do not join two independent clauses with only a comma; use a period, semicolon, or conjunction with comma.

examples.wrong: I went home, it was late.
examples.correct: I went home. It was late.

rules.title: semicolon misuse
rules.rule: Use semicolons between related independent clauses, not to replace a comma before a dependent phrase.

examples.wrong: I went to the store; to buy apples.
examples.correct: I went to the store to buy apples.

rules.title: apostrophe for plurals
rules.rule: Do not use apostrophes for simple plurals; use apostrophes for possession or contractions.

rules.title: exclamation marks
rules.rule: Use one exclamation mark sparingly; avoid multiple !!! in formal writing.

rules.title: question mark
rules.rule: Direct questions end with ? not a period.

rules.title: comma after introductory phrase
rules.rule: Often use a comma after long introductory adverbs or clauses.

rules.title: lists and commas
rules.rule: In a series of three or more, use commas between items; optional Oxford comma before and.

rules.title: quotation punctuation
rules.rule: In American English, periods and commas go inside closing quotation marks.

rules.title: capitalization after period
rules.rule: Capitalize the first word of a new sentence after a period.

examples.wrong: we went back. to our house.
examples.correct: we went back. To our house.

rules.title: sentence fragments
rules.rule: A standalone dependent phrase like 'To our house.' is a fragment unless connected to a main clause.

rules.title: subject-verb agreement (number)
rules.rule: Singular subjects take singular verbs; plural subjects take plural verbs. 'He and I' is plural → use 'were'.

rules.title: collective nouns
rules.rule: Collective nouns and 'everyone' usually take singular verbs in American English.

rules.title: there is / there are
rules.rule: Use 'there is' with singular and 'there are' with plural following nouns.

rules.title: each / every / neither
rules.rule: Singular indefinite pronouns (each, every, everyone) take singular verbs.

rules.title: third person singular -s
rules.rule: Present tense third-person singular adds -s or -es (he walks, she gives).

rules.title: don't vs doesn't
rules.rule: Use doesn't with he/she/it; don't with I/you/we/they.

rules.title: subject pronoun order
rules.rule: Put yourself last and use subject pronouns (I, he, she) as subjects: 'He and I went', NEVER 'Me and him went'. Compound subjects take 'were' not 'was'.

rules.title: object pronoun order
rules.rule: After prepositions use object pronouns: between you and me, for him and her.

rules.title: reflexive pronouns
rules.rule: Use reflexive pronouns only when subject and object are the same; no 'theirself'.

rules.title: a / an articles
rules.rule: Use 'an' before vowel sounds; 'a' before consonant sounds.

rules.title: adverb vs adjective
rules.rule: Adverbs modify verbs (rains heavily); adjectives modify nouns (heavy rain).

rules.title: irregular past tense
rules.rule: Common irregular verbs: go→went, catch→caught, buy→bought, run→ran.

rules.title: double negatives
rules.rule: Avoid double negatives in standard English (don't have no → don't have any).

rules.title: gerund vs infinitive
rules.rule: Some verbs take gerund (-ing) or infinitive (to) depending on meaning; check common patterns.

rules.title: modal verbs
rules.rule: After modals use base verb without 'to' (should go, not should to go).

rules.title: comparatives
rules.rule: Do not double comparatives (more better → better; more fast → faster).

rules.title: plural nouns
rules.rule: Use correct plural forms (packages, children, not childs or package for plural).

rules.title: because / because of
rules.rule: 'Because' introduces a clause (because we had); 'because of' + noun phrase (because of rain).

rules.title: upstairs / up the stairs
rules.rule: 'Upstairs' is an adverb; 'up the stairs' is preposition + noun when movement is explicit.

examples.wrong: walk up the stairs (when adverb intended)

rules.title: separate
rules.rule: Correct spelling is separate (not seperate).

rules.title: occurred / occurring
rules.rule: Double r in occurred and occurring.

rules.title: accommodate
rules.rule: Correct spelling is accommodate (double c and double m).

rules.title: i before e
rules.rule: i before e except after c: receive, believe (watch weird exceptions).

rules.title: tomorrow
rules.rule: Correct spelling is tomorrow (one m, two r's).
